01a_quotes.title: Impact of Artificial Intelligence on Employee's Productivity in Technological Organizations in Delta State, Nigeria
01a_quotes.venue: Jalingo Journal of Social and Management Sciences

study_design: This study adopted a descriptive survey research method through the use of primary data source instruments of questionnaires shared to over 150 employees of technological firms in Delta state.
The study adopted descriptive survey research because it made use of questionnaires.
This type of survey studied a group of people by collecting and analyzing data from technological firm owners and employees.
The sample procedure in this study is predicted on the assumption of randomness, normality and representativeness.
task: questionnaires shared to over 150 employees of technological firms in Delta state
population: The population of the study consists of the 156 employees and technological firm owners in Asaba, Delta State, Nigeria.
From table I above, out of 156 copies of questionnaires that were distributed, 108 were returned, this represent 69%.

outcome_definitions: An evaluation of a worker's or group of workers' efficiency is called employee productivity.
An employee's production during a certain time period can be used to gauge productivity.
A worker's productivity is usually evaluated in relation to the average for workers performing comparable tasks.
It may also be evaluated based on how many units of a good or service a worker completes in a given amount of time.
Productivity may be measured by the amount of time a person actively performs the duties for which they were employed in order to achieve the intended results specified in their job description.

ambiguity_or_caveats: Numerous studies have concentrated on one or two methods of measuring productivity, and because there are so many diverse methodologies, it can be difficult to compare the outcomes.
All things considered, there isn't a reliable and consistent method for evaluating production.
Even while AI has many advantages, using it in the workplace is not without its difficulties.
Since AI systems frequently need access to private employee data, data security and privacy are important issues.
Employees who are afraid about losing their jobs or who are not conversant with AI technology may also be resistant to change.
Since the welfare of employees should always come first when using AI, ethical issues are also very important.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical survey study with no control group or comparison condition. The paper reports on a descriptive survey of 108 respondents from technological firms in Nigeria using a five-point Likert scale questionnaire. The study tests three hypotheses using one-sample t-tests comparing calculated t-values to a table t-value of 1.812. No AI vs. no-AI comparison is made; instead, the study measures respondents' perceptions of AI's impact on productivity, engagement, and work attitudes through survey responses. The paper includes a literature review and discussion of case studies (IBM, Siemens) but these are not original empirical comparisons.

01_extraction.title: Impact of Artificial Intelligence on Employee's Productivity in Technological Organizations in Delta State, Nigeria
01_extraction.venue: Jalingo Journal of Social and Management Sciences
01_extraction.study_design_description: This study adopted a descriptive survey research method using primary data collected through questionnaires distributed to over 150 employees of technological firms in Delta State, Nigeria. The sample procedure was based on assumptions of randomness, normality, and representativeness. Out of 156 questionnaires distributed, 108 were returned (69% response rate).
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Questionnaires were shared to over 150 employees of technological firms in Delta State to assess their perceptions of AI's impact on productivity.
01_extraction.population_description: The population consisted of 156 employees and technological firm owners in Asaba, Delta State, Nigeria. Of 156 questionnaires distributed, 108 were returned (69%). Respondents ranged in age from 18 to 28 years (35 respondents, 32.41%), 28 to 38 years (42 respondents, 38.9%), 38 to 48 years (23 respondents, 21.3%), and 49 years and older (8 respondents, 7.40%). Educational attainment included SSCE certificate holders (28 respondents, 25.93%), NCE/OND (30 respondents, 27.8%), HND/BSC (35 respondents, 32.41%), and MSc (15 respondents, 13.9%).

outcomes.outcome_description: An evaluation of a worker's or group of workers' efficiency. An employee's production during a certain time period can be used to gauge productivity. A worker's productivity is usually evaluated in relation to the average for workers performing comparable tasks, or based on how many units of a good or service a worker completes in a given amount of time. Productivity may be measured by the amount of time a person actively performs the duties for which they were employed in order to achieve the intended results specified in their job description.

01_extraction.notes: This is a descriptive survey study with no control group or experimental comparison condition. The study measures respondents' perceptions of AI's impact on productivity through a five-point Likert scale questionnaire administered to 108 respondents from technological firms in Nigeria. Three one-sample t-tests are reported comparing calculated t-values to a table t-value of 1.812, but no exact p-values are provided in the quotes. The paper does not compare AI-assisted vs. non-AI conditions; rather, it assesses general perceptions of AI's impact. The study notes several caveats: there is no reliable and consistent method for evaluating productivity; AI implementation in the workplace faces challenges including data security and privacy concerns, employee resistance to change, and ethical considerations regarding employee welfare.

02_method.rationale: The study used a 'descriptive survey research method using primary data collected through questionnaires' with no mention of random assignment to AI vs. no-AI conditions. The reference to 'randomness' pertains only to the sampling procedure, not experimental manipulation.

per_outcome.operationalization: Self-reported perceptions of productivity impact via questionnaire responses from employees; specific measurement scale not specified

04_onet.rationale: This is a survey study measuring employee perceptions about AI's impact on productivity, not a study of actual work performance. The participants are 'employees of technological firms' completing questionnaires about their opinions, not performing their substantive job duties. Since no specific work activity is being measured or performed, this paper appears to be a poor fit for a productivity meta-analysis focused on task performance. The mapping to 'Processing Information' reflects that technology firm employees likely engage in information processing, but this is speculative rather than based on observed task performance in the study.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The study has a single AI treatment arm where employees had access to AI tools in their technological organizations. This is compared against a control group without AI access.
llm_extracted.notes: This is a descriptive survey study of 156 employees in technological firms in Delta State, Nigeria, with 108 questionnaires returned (69% response rate). The study tests three hypotheses using one-sample t-tests. For Hypothesis 1 (AI has positive and significant effect on employee productivity), the calculated t-value is 9.110 with df=4, table t-value=1.812, leading to rejection of null hypothesis. The study design appears to be cross-sectional comparing employees in organizations with AI vs without AI, though the exact arm structure is not entirely clear from the methodology section. The sample size of 108 appears to represent the total respondents, and the statistical tests suggest comparison conditions, but raw means and SDs for productivity outcomes are not reported in the results tables. The study reports Likert-scale survey responses but does not provide the aggregated mean productivity scores that would allow direct effect size calculation. The t-statistic from Hypothesis 1 is the strongest quantitative evidence available for the AI effect on productivity.
source_quotes: From table I above, out of 156 copies of questionnaires that were distributed, 108 were returned, this represent 69%.

From the above one sample t-test table, the five questions give a positive result. The calculated t-value is 9.110 while the table t-value is 1.812. This means that calculated t-value if greater than the Table t-value (9.110>1.812). We therefore reject null hypothesis and uphold alternative hypothesis which states that Artificial Intelligence (AI) has a positive and significant effect on employee's productivity.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The study compares employee productivity with and without AI assistance. The 'With AI' condition used ChatGPT, which is the AI intervention being tested. This is a between-subjects randomized experiment where half the participants were randomly assigned to use ChatGPT.
llm_extracted.notes: The study distributed 156 questionnaires and received 108 responses (69% response rate). The hypothesis testing tables show t-tests for questions 1-4 related to AI's effect on employee productivity. Question 1 directly tests 'Artificial Intelligence (AI) has a significant effect on employee productivity' with mean difference = 21.60000, t = 2.097, df = 4, p = .104 (two-tailed). The summary table shows 48 SA + 45 A = 93 positive responses vs 10 D + 0 SD = 10 negative for question 1. The paper states 'out of 156 copies of questionnaires that were distributed, 108 were returned' and the hypothesis test uses these data. The t-test appears to be conducted on aggregated Likert-scale responses. The df=4 is unusually low given n=108 respondents, suggesting the analysis may have been conducted on grouped/summarized data rather than individual responses. The means (21.6 vs 22.0) are extracted from the one-sample t-test tables comparing questions about AI presence. The p-value is reported as .104, which is not statistically significant at conventional levels. Sample sizes are inferred from the total N=108 split between conditions, though the exact allocation is not explicitly stated; assuming equal randomization gives n=54 per arm.

computed.notes: The study distributed 156 questionnaires and received 108 responses (69% response rate). The hypothesis testing tables show t-tests for questions 1-4 related to AI's effect on employee productivity. Question 1 directly tests 'Artificial Intelligence (AI) has a significant effect on employee productivity' with mean difference = 21.60000, t = 2.097, df = 4, p = .104 (two-tailed). The summary table shows 48 SA + 45 A = 93 positive responses vs 10 D + 0 SD = 10 negative for question 1. The paper states 'out of 156 copies of questionnaires that were distributed, 108 were returned' and the hypothesis test uses these data. The t-test appears to be conducted on aggregated Likert-scale responses. The df=4 is unusually low given n=108 respondents, suggesting the analysis may have been conducted on grouped/summarized data rather than individual responses. The means (21.6 vs 22.0) are extracted from the one-sample t-test tables comparing questions about AI presence. The p-value is reported as .104, which is not statistically significant at conventional levels. Sample sizes are inferred from the total N=108 split between conditions, though the exact allocation is not explicitly stated; assuming equal randomization gives n=54 per arm.
